{{Hatnote|Not to be confused with [[Communitas]] the esoteric aspect of the Pride}}
[[File:Heart.svg|thumb|Aspect symbol]]
[[File:Lion cub with mother.jpg|thumb|A Pride of Lions]]
The Pride is one of the teeth of the [[Sevenfold Vaccine]]. 

It is the [[booster shot]] for [[alienation]]. In this respect it is attempting to cultivate [[communitas]] which is seen as the esoteric manifestation of the pride.

It represents the community of [[Evolutionists]] especially at a local level. Local parishes are called Prides, while the whole organization is called the [[Church of Evolution|church]].

Prides meet up for [[Sanhedrin]] and for [[Activities]] including [[volunteering]], recreational activities, and [[classes]].

The name comes from a Pride of Lions, and from [[Aristotle's Lion]]

[[Category:The Pride]]
[[Category:Alienation]]
[[Category:Ninefold vaccine]]
[[sn:Pride]]==In Catgirl Simulator==
In the game [[Catgirl Simulator]], {{PAGENAME}} is a core mechanic, deriving its origin from one of the [[Nine Aspects]], an integral system borrowed from Cultist Simulator. This aspect is specifically tied to a portion of the [[Ninefold Vaccine]], forming a critical component of the game.

The Nine Aspects in [[Catgirl Simulator]] don't encompass all elements from Cultist Simulator; notably, tools are absent. However, each Aspect manifests through 12 distinct lore fragments, 4 levels of influence, and a minimum of 12 associated books, providing at least one book per lore level.

In this specific context, the {{PAGENAME}} aligns with '''[[The Pride]] ([[Alienation]] Vaccine) and [[Heart]] Aspect'''. The Heart aspect encapsulates vitality, emotions, and human connectivity, making it an apt fit for The Pride, a vaccine devised to counter Alienation. As the Heart draws on the potency of human bonds and emotions, The Pride works to rekindle our sense of belonging and connection, reinforcing unique personal qualities, bridging the gap caused by alienation, and restoring community cohesion.

===Lore Fragments===
Lore fragments are elements of ancient wisdom, mysticism, and knowledge that players can collect throughout their journey. Each fragment has a unique ID, name, and description, providing hints or guiding the player's actions. The Pride and Heart Aspect has 12 associated lore fragments:

* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>A Leader's Secret<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheart): "The roar of unity echoes in every lionheart. Let us recognize this shared pulse."
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Iron Lion Zion<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheartb): "The syllables of this chant hold a compelling rhythm, mirroring the steady beat of a lionheart. It yearns for the continuity of the pride."
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Pride's Anthem<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheartc): "Who could hear this and remain untouched? The lone? The lost? The land and the heavens? [Use this in a Rite with Byzantine Tinct and a Meteoric Bullet - or other resources of equal power - to summon a Percussigant.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Twinned Roars<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheartd): "The Lioness-and-King has always been invoked for protection, at crossroads, moonrise, and where the land cradles the sea. [Use this in a Rite with Byzantine Tinct and a Meteoric Bullet - or other resources of equal power - to summon a Percussigant.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Rhythm of the Watchful<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmenthearte): "One who watches over the pride does not sleep; one who sleeps is not the watcher. <nowiki><b>One who watches over the pride does not sleep; one who sleeps is not the watcher.</b></nowiki> [Use this in a Rite with Byzantine Tinct and a Meteoric Bullet - or other resources of equal power - to summon a Percussigant.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Sanctuary of the Strong<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheartf): "Within the firm 'No' of the Pride lies a refuge—an impenetrable fortress sheltering those within from harm. [Use this in a Rite with Byzantine Tinct and a Meteoric Bullet - or other resources of equal power - to summon a Percussigant.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Unfathomable Prowess<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (fragmentheartg): "The Grail marked the Lionheart with its roar. The Lioness-and-King emerged from two lairs. The Pride awoke when the land tasted the strength of unity. The Heart is the beat of the Pride, and here is its sacred doctrine... [Use this in a Rite with Byzantine Tinct and a Meteoric Bullet - or other resources of equal power - to summon a Percussigant.]"


===Influences===
Influences in Catgirl Simulator are elements that shape the game environment, affecting various aspects of gameplay. The Pride and Heart Aspect consists of 4 influence levels:

* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Resonant Pulse<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (influenceheart): "The heart's rhythm echoes in a symphony of unity. [A second-order influence: a spark of emotion and human connection, reminiscent of The Pride's fight against Alienation. This influence can be used in rites to summon certain entities.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Shared Heartbeat<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (influenceheartc): "A vibrant, collective rhythm undulates through existence. [A sixth-order influence: The Pride's influence strengthens, fostering community and unity. Useful in rites to summon specific entities.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Pride Manifest<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (influencehearte): "The collective pulse resonates with pride, bridging alienation and restoring unity. [A tenth-order influence! A surge of shared emotion and connection. This influence can be used in rites to summon powerful entities.]"
* <nowiki>'''</nowiki>Unity's Triumph<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (influenceheartg): "Every heartbeat sings in unison. Alienation fades into a distant memory. [A fifteenth-order influence! This might be the attention of The Pride itself. Powerful enough to summon the most potent entities.]"

===Books===

*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Book of Joy: Lasting Happiness in a Changing World<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookhearta): This book is a profound and inspiring collaboration between two of the world's most spiritually influential figures, His Holiness the Dalai Lama (Tenzin Gyatso) and Archbishop Desmond Tutu. Compiled and co-authored by Douglas Abrams, the book records a week-long dialogue between the Dalai Lama and Archbishop Tutu in Dharamsala, India, where they discuss one of life's most fundamental quests: finding lasting joy in the face of life's inevitable suffering.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Four Loves<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookheartb): In this book, C.S. Lewis explores the nature of love from a Christian and philosophical perspective. He categorizes love into four types—Affection, Friendship, Eros, and Charity—and reflects upon their role in our lives, providing deep insights into the complexities and significance of human relationships.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>A Theory of Justice<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookheartc): This is a seminal work by John Rawls in modern political philosophy. The book provides an alternative to utilitarianism and intuitionism, proposing a conception of justice as fairness, and offering a comprehensive discussion of distributive justice.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookheartd): This work by Karl Marx is a series of notes that express the author's ideas on alienation, the state of the worker, and the evolution of capitalism. It contains the core ideas that later became the backbone of Marx's masterpieces, Capital and The Communist Manifesto.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Analects<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookhearte): This is a collection of sayings and ideas attributed to the Chinese philosopher Confucius and his contemporaries. Considered one of the central texts of Confucianism, it offers deep insights into personal morality, ethical conduct, and the proper exercise of political power.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Theory of Moral Sentiments<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookheartf): In this work, Adam Smith presents a comprehensive discussion of the nature of morality, virtue, and the motivations behind human actions. Known for his economic theories, Smith delves into moral philosophy in this work, exploring how mutual sympathy forms the basis of moral sentiments.
*<nowiki>'''</nowiki>The Social Contract<nowiki>'''</nowiki> (bookheartg): This is a fundamental work by Jean-Jacques Rousseau in political philosophy. The book examines the concept of a legitimate political authority within the context of classical republicanism. Rousseau posits that a just society is guided by the 'general will' of its citizenry, an idea that has greatly influenced modern political thought.

==Etymology==


The name comes from a Pride of Lions, and from [[Aristotle's Lion]], and from gay pride

==As a virtue==
As a virtue Pride represents the ability to authentically interact with people and express solidarity

==Pride Structure==
'''The Pride''' represents the community of [[Evolutionists]] especially at a local level. Local parishes are called Prides, while the whole organization is called the [[School of Evolution|school]].

Prides meet up for [[Sanhedrin]] and for [[Activities]] including [[volunteering]], recreational activities, and [[classes]].

==Further reading==

*[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RZvBadGqEbM explained well in this stream] around 7 minutes in

[[Category:The Pride]]
[[Category:Alienation]]

==Wikipedia==

{{for|the book by Percival and Paul Goodman|Communitas (book)}}
{{Anthropology of religion|Basic|image=[[File:Roman fresco Villa dei Misteri Pompeii 001.jpg|260px]]|caption=Initiation at the Villa of Mysteries, Pompeii, a rite of passage expressing communitas}}
'''''Communitas''''' is a [[Latin]] [[noun]] commonly referring either to an unstructured [[community]] in which [[person|people]] are equal, or to the very spirit of community. It also has special significance as a [[loanword]] in [[cultural anthropology]] and the [[social sciences]]. [[Victor Turner]], who defined the anthropological usage of communitas, was interested in the interplay between what he called social 'structure' and 'antistructure'; ''Liminality'' and ''Communitas'' are both components of antistructure.<ref>Turner, V. (1974). Dramas, Fields, and Metaphors: Symbolic Action in Human Society. Cornell University Press. pp. 273-4.</ref>

Communitas refers to an unstructured state in which all members of a community are equal allowing them to share a common experience, usually through a rite of passage. Communitas is characteristic of people experiencing [[liminality]] together. This term is used to distinguish the modality of social [[Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] from an area of common living. There is more than one distinction between [[structure]] and communitas. The most familiar is the difference of [[secular]] and [[sacred]]. Every social position has something sacred about it. This sacred component is acquired during [[rite of passage|rites of passages]], through the changing of positions. Part of this sacredness is achieved through the transient humility learned in these phases, this allows people to reach a higher position.

==Victor and Edith Turner==
Communitas is an acute point of community. It takes community to the next level and allows the whole of the community to share a common experience, usually through a rite of passage. This brings everyone onto an equal level: even if you are higher in position, you have been lower and you know what that is.

Turner (1969, Pg.132; see also <ref>Olaveson, T. (2001). Collective Effervescence and Communitas: Processual Models of Ritual and Society in Emile Durkheim and Victor Turner. Dialectical Anthropology, 26, pp. 105.</ref>) distinguishes between:
*''existential or spontaneous communitas'', the transient personal experience of togetherness; e.g. that which occurs during a counter-culture happening.
*''normative communitas'', which occurs as communitas is transformed from its existential state to being organized into a permanent social system due to the need for social control.
*''ideological communitas'', which can be applied to many utopian social models.

Communitas as a concept used by [[Victor Turner]] in his study of [[ritual]] has been criticized by anthropologists. See [[John Eade]] & [[Michael J. Sallnow]]'s ''[[Contesting the Sacred]]'' (1991)

Edith Turner, Victor's widow and anthropologist in her own right, published in 2011<ref>Turner, Edith. 2012. ''Communitas: The Anthropology of Collective Joy''. New York: Palgrave Macmillan.</ref> a definitive overview of the anthropology of communitas, outlining the concept in relation to the natural history of joy, including the nature of human experience and its narration, festivals, music and sports, work, disaster, the sacred, revolution and nonviolence, nature and spirit, and ritual and rites of passage.

==Paul and Percival Goodman==
{{main article|Communitas (book)}}
''[[Communitas (book)|Communitas]]'' is also the title of a book published in 1947 by the 20th-century American thinker and writer [[Paul Goodman (writer)|Paul Goodman]] and his brother, [[Percival Goodman]]. Their book examines three kinds of possible societies: a society centered on consumption, a society centered on artistic and creative pursuits, and a society which maximizes human liberty. The Goodmans emphasize freedom from both coercion by a government or church and from human necessities by providing these free of cost to all citizens who do a couple of years of conscripted labor as young adults.

==Roberto Esposito==
In 1998, Italian philosopher [[Roberto Esposito]] published a book under the name ''Communitas'' challenging the traditional understanding of this concept. It was translated in English in 2010 by Timothy Campbell. In this book, Esposito offers a very different interpretation of the concept of ''communitas'' based on a thorough etymological analysis of the word: "Community isn't a property, nor is it a territory to be separated and defended against those who do not belong to it. Rather, it is a void, a debt, a gift to the other that also reminds us of our constitutive alterity with respect to ourselves."<ref>[http://www.sup.org/book.cgi?id=4107 Stanford University Press] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100613225247/http://www.sup.org/book.cgi?id=4107 |date=2010-06-13 }} : ''Communitas. The Origin and Destiny of Community'' by Roberto Esposito, translated by Timothy C. Campbell</ref> He goes on with his "deconstruction" of the concept of ''communitas'': <blockquote>"From here it emerges that ''communitas'' is the totality of persons united not by a "property" but precisely by an obligation or a debt; not by an "addition" but by a "subtraction": by a lack, a limit that is configured as an onus, or even as a defective modality for him who is "affected", unlike for him who is instead "exempt" or "exempted". Here we find the final and most characteristic of the oppositions associated with (or that dominate) the alternative between public and private, those in other words that contrast ''communitas'' to ''immunitas''. If ''communis'' is he who is required to carry out the functions of an office ― or to the donation of a grace ― on the contrary, he is called immune who has to perform no office, and for that reason he remains ungrateful. He can completely preserve his own position through a ''vacatio muneris''. Whereas the ''communitas'' is bound by the sacrifice of the ''compensatio'', the ''immunitas'' implies the beneficiary of the ''dispensatio''."<ref>ESPOSITO, Roberto ([1998]2010). ''Communitas. The Origin and Destiny of Community'', tr. by Timothy Campbell, Stanford: Stanford University Press, p.6</ref></blockquote><blockquote>"Therefore the community cannot be thought of as a body, as a corporation in which individuals are founded in a larger individual. Neither is community to be interpreted as a mutual, intersubjective "recognition" in which individuals are reflected in each other so as to confirm their initial identity; as a collective bond that comes at a certain point to connect individuals that before were separate. The community isn't a mode of being, much less a "making" of the individual subject. It isn't the subject's expansion or multiplication but its exposure to what interrupts the closing and turns it inside out: a dizziness, a syncope, a spasm in the continuity of the subject."<ref>ESPOSITO, Roberto ([1998]2010). ''Communitas. The Origin and Destiny of Community'', tr. by Timothy Campbell, Stanford: Stanford University Press, p.7</ref></blockquote>

==Others==
For more on this perspective, see also [[Jean-Luc Nancy]]'s paper "The Confronted Community"<ref>NANCY, Jean-Luc ([2001]2003). [https://www.scribd.com/doc/21696686/The-Confronted-Community-by-Jean-Luc-Nancy "The Confronted Community"], ''Postcolonial Studies'', Vol. 6 no. 1, pp. 23-36</ref> as well as his book [[Jean-Luc Nancy#La Communauté dés.C5.93uvrée|''The Inoperative Community'']].<ref>NANCY, Jean-Luc ([1983] 1991). ''The Inoperative Community'', Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press. This essay was first published in the French magazine ''Aléa'', which was edited at that time by Christian Bourgois. Cf. Jean-Luc Nancy, ''La communauté désoeuvrée'', Paris: Christian Bourgois, 1983</ref> See also [[Maurice Blanchot]]'s book ''The Unavowable Community'' (1983)<ref>BLANCHOT, Maurice ([1983]1988). 'The Unavowable Community', Station Hill Press</ref> which is an answer to [[Jean-Luc Nancy]]'s ''Inoperative Community''. [[Giorgio Agamben]] engages in a similar argument about the concept of community in his 1990 book [[Giorgio Agamben#The Coming Community (1993)|''The Coming Community'']] (translated in English by [[Michael Hardt]] in 1993).<ref>AGAMBEN, Giorgio ([1990]1993). ''The Coming Community'', Minnesota: University of Minnesota Press, 1993 [https://books.google.com/books?id=6ekx1dg4nSgC Google Books Preview]</ref> Rémi Astruc, a French scholar, recently proposed in his essay ''Nous? L'aspiration à la Communauté et les arts'' (2015),<ref>ASTRUC, Rémi (2015) ''Nous? L'aspiration à la Communauté et les arts'', Versailles, RKI Press</ref> to operate a distinction between Community with a capital C as the longing for ''communitas'' and communities (plural and small c) to name the numerous actualizations in human societies. Finally, on the American side, see ''The Community of Those Who Have Nothing in Common'' by [[Alphonso Lingis]].<ref>LINGIS, Alphonso (1994). ''The Community of Those Who Have Nothing in Common'', Indiana: Indiana University Press. [https://books.google.com/books?id=arI1kHTxQTMC Google Books Preview]</ref> Christian author [[Alan Hirsch]] used the term to describe a more active, tighter-knit community in his book "The Forgotten Ways: Reactivating the Missional Church."[https://www.cmaresources.org/video/communitas_alan-hirsch]

==References==
<references />

==Further reading==
*Read the introduction from Roberto Esposito's book ''Communitas. The Origin and Destiny of Community'' : [https://www.sup.org/books/extra/?id=4107&i=Introduction_pages&p=1 Introduction: Nothing In Common]
*[[Victor Turner|Turner, Victor]]. "[[Rituals and Communitas]]." Creative Resistance. 26 Nov. 2005
*Eade & Sallnow, 'Contesting the Sacred' (1991)
*Carse, James P. "The Religious Case Against Belief", Penguin, New York, 2008

[[Category:Community]]
[[Category:Spirituality]]
[[Category:Latin words and phrases]]
[[Category:Booster shots]]
[[Category:Sacredness]]

[[es:Communitas]]
[[Category:Pages]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
